question: How hot does compost pile get?
title: How Hot Does a Compost Pile Get? The Science of Thermophilic Decomposition
metaDescription: A well-built compost pile can hit 150°F or higher. Scotty explains what's driving that heat, why it matters for your soil, and how to know if your pile is hot enough.
content:
I've had my hot compost pile up to 150 degrees Fahrenheit. I know because I have a thermometer I stick in it.

The first time I watched that thermometer climb past 130, then 140, then nudge toward 150 — I'll tell you, I got excited. That is not an exaggeration. Because what that temperature tells you is that you have built conditions for the most biologically active thing that can happen to a pile of organic matter outside a living digestive system. You have unleashed thermophilic bacteria.

Most people don't know their compost pile can get that hot. Most people don't know their compost pile is supposed to get that hot. Let me explain what's happening and why it matters.

---

## What Temperature Does a Compost Pile Reach?

A properly built, actively managed hot compost pile can reach temperatures between 130 and 160 degrees Fahrenheit in the thermophilic phase. Cornell Composting's research documents that commercial and well-managed municipal compost systems regularly reach 60 to 70 degrees Celsius — that's 140 to 158 degrees Fahrenheit.

For a backyard pile, 130 to 150°F is a realistic target. If you've built your pile right — the right carbon-to-nitrogen ratio, the right moisture, enough mass to hold heat — you can get there within two to three days of building the pile.

For reference, 130°F is hot enough to pasteurize liquid. It is hot enough to kill most weed seeds. It is hot enough to eliminate the majority of human and plant pathogens. This is not a mild biological process. This is serious heat generated by billions of bacteria metabolizing organic carbon at maximum efficiency.

## The Three Temperature Phases

A hot compost pile moves through distinct temperature phases, each dominated by different microbial communities. Understanding those phases helps you manage the pile better.

**Mesophilic phase (room temperature to ~110°F):** This is the warm-up. Mesophilic bacteria — organisms that prefer moderate temperatures — move in first and begin breaking down the readily available sugars, starches, and simple organic compounds. They multiply rapidly. As they metabolize, they generate heat. The pile starts warming from the inside out, sometimes within the first 12 to 24 hours of being built.

**Thermophilic phase (110°F to ~160°F):** This is where the action is. When the pile gets too hot for mesophilic bacteria, thermophilic bacteria take over. These organisms have evolved for high heat — they prefer temperatures between 104 and 140°F, and some specialized species remain active up to 160°F. Research published by UF/IFAS extension confirms that thermophilic bacteria like Bacillus stearothermophilus and related species dominate this phase and are responsible for the rapid breakdown of proteins, fats, and complex carbohydrates. This is when you see steam coming off the pile when you pitch it. This is when the pile shrinks most dramatically.

**Cooling and curing phase (dropping back below 110°F):** As the readily available carbon gets used up, the thermophilic bacteria run out of fuel and the pile cools. Mesophilic bacteria return. Fungi and actinomycetes move in to tackle the tougher lignin and cellulose compounds. Worms eventually join the party if conditions are right. The pile stabilizes into finished compost.

## Why Heat Matters: Pathogen Kill and Weed Seeds

Here's the practical payoff of getting your pile hot: safety and seed management.

Food scraps, manure, and yard waste can carry plant pathogens, human pathogens, and weed seeds. At 130°F sustained for several days, the EPA and USDA guidelines confirm that most pathogens — including E. coli, Salmonella, and most plant disease organisms — are eliminated. At 140°F and above, the kill is more thorough and faster.

Weed seeds are similarly vulnerable to heat. Most weed seeds are killed at temperatures above 130°F sustained for a few days. This is why a properly hot-composted pile produces compost you can put on your beds without worrying about introducing new weeds from your inputs.

Cold composting — the slow pile you don't manage — does not reliably reach these temperatures. It may eventually produce compost, but it will not kill weed seeds and pathogens with the same reliability. If you're using manure from your own animals or inputs from unknown sources, a hot pile is not optional — it is a biosafety step.

## How to Build a Pile That Gets Hot

Temperature is the result of several inputs coming together correctly. Here's what matters.

**Mass.** A pile needs enough mass to insulate its own core. The minimum size for a hot pile is roughly three feet in each dimension — height, width, depth. Below that threshold, the heat dissipates through the outer surface too quickly for the thermophilic phase to build. Bigger is generally better, up to about five feet, where you start getting airflow problems in the center.

**Carbon and nitrogen balance.** The 25-to-1 carbon-to-nitrogen ratio is the fuel mix for thermophilic bacteria. Too much carbon and the pile is slow and cold — the bacteria can't multiply fast enough to generate heat. Too much nitrogen and you get ammonia off-gassing instead of biological heat. Get the ratio right and the pile will light off on its own.

**Moisture.** The pile should feel like a wrung-out sponge — moist throughout but not dripping. Dry piles don't heat. Waterlogged piles go anaerobic and lose heat from oxygen deprivation.

**Aeration.** Turn the pile. When a hot pile uses up the oxygen in its core, it cools down. Turning reintroduces oxygen and reignites the thermophilic activity. I turn my active piles at least once a week, sometimes twice during the first two weeks when the activity is highest. Every turn is an invitation for another heat spike.

## Reading Your Pile With a Thermometer

Get a compost thermometer — the long-probe type that reaches into the center of the pile. Stick it in twelve to eighteen inches and check the reading.

Below 90°F: the pile is cold, probably too dry or too carbon-heavy.
90 to 110°F: mesophilic phase, warming up. Check moisture and inputs.
110 to 150°F: thermophilic phase. This is where you want it.
Above 160°F: too hot. The beneficial thermophilic bacteria are dying off and only the most extreme heat-tolerant species remain. Turn the pile immediately to cool it down and reintroduce oxygen.

After a few weeks of active management, when the pile stops reheating after turning — staying below 110°F regardless of what you do — the thermophilic phase is complete. The pile needs to cure. Give it another few weeks, and you'll have finished compost.

## Why the Heat Matters Beyond Decomposition Speed

I want to dwell on something, because it connects composting to a broader idea about soil biology and the decay cycle.

When Gabe Brown talks about the role of the producer in managing soil biology, he's describing a system where the farmer or gardener is not the primary actor — the biology is. The farmer's job is to create conditions where the biology can do its work. Hot composting is one of the clearest expressions of that principle.

You are not making the compost. The thermophilic bacteria are making the compost. You build the pile with the right inputs and structure, you provide oxygen through turning, you maintain moisture — and then you step back and let the microbial community do what it does. The heat is their signature. The steam is their work being done.

Albert Howard observed this about composting in his work at Indore in India in the 1930s, where he developed the Indore composting method. He was watching microbial communities at work and understanding that the process was biological, not chemical. The job of the composter was to understand the biology and manage the conditions — not to add chemicals or apply force, but to create the environment where the decay cycle could run efficiently.

That is still the right framework. Hot composting is managed ecology. The thermometer tells you how well the ecology is functioning. When the pile hits 150 degrees, you know the conditions are right and the community is thriving. When it drops off, you adjust — turn the pile, add nitrogen if the heat has stalled, add moisture if it's dry. The biology responds.

That feedback loop is one of the best things about hot composting as a practice. You are watching biology work in real time, in your own backyard. You learn to read a pile. You understand what heat and steam and smell and color are telling you about the state of a microbial community. And that understanding translates directly to understanding your garden soil — because the same principles apply.

The heat was the thing. It was always the heat.

---

scottyQuotes: I had my hot compost pile all the way up to 150 degrees. When you hit that, you know the biology is really working.
It was 140 about a week ago and now it's creeping up. When the pile is very hot like that, that's a great indicator that the bacteria are doing their job.
There's a ton of steam coming off this pile. This is a very hot, active bacterial breakdown.
